perturbation.original_text: The Adviser hereby agrees to indemnify and save harmless FASC and its trustees, officers and employees (each of the foregoing an "Indemnified Party" and collectively the "Indemnified Parties") against and from any and all losses, obligations, penalties, actions, judgments and suits and other costs, expenses and disbursements of any kind or nature whatsoever which may be imposed on, incurred by or asserted against an Indemnified Party, other than as a consequence of gross negligence or willful misconduct on the part of an Indemnified Party, arising out of or in connection with this Limited Power of Attorney or any other agreement, instrument or document executed in connection with the exercise of the authority granted to FASC herein to act on behalf of the Adviser, including without limitation the reasonable costs, expenses and disbursements in connection with defending such Indemnified Party against any claim or liability related to the exercise or performance of any of FASC's powers or duties under this Limited Power of Attorney or any of the other agreements, instruments or documents executed in connection with the exercise of the authority granted to FASC herein to act on behalf of the Adviser, or the taking of any action under or in connection with any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: The Adviser agrees to potentially indemnify FASC and its trustees, officers, and employees (each an "Indemnified Party" and collectively the "Indemnified Parties") against some losses, obligations, penalties, actions, judgments, and suits, and certain costs, expenses, and disbursements which may, potentially, be imposed on, incurred by, or asserted against an Indemnified Party, except possibly for consequences of gross negligence or willful misconduct by the Indemnified Party, connected to this Limited Power of Attorney or related agreements. The determination of indemnification shall be at the Adviser's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces significant ambiguity. Replacing 'agrees to indemnify' with 'agrees to potentially indemnify' and adding qualifiers like 'some', 'certain', 'potentially' weakens the obligation. The addition of 'The determination of indemnification shall be at the Adviser's sole discretion' further undermines the indemnification clause, making it unenforceable and discretionary rather than obligatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17(h) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 prohibits exculpation of investment company personnel for actions constituting willful misfeasance, bad faith, gross negligence, or reckless disregard of duty. By making indemnification discretionary and weakening the language regarding gross negligence, the modified text creates ambiguity which could be interpreted as attempting to limit liability for actions that should not be exculpated under the Investment Company Act.
perturbation.location: Paragraph starting with 'The Adviser hereby agrees to indemnify'

Transactions of certain affiliated persons and underwriters (a) Prohibited transactions It shall be unlawful for any affiliated person or promoter of or principal underwriter for a registered investment company (other than a company of the character described in section 80a–12(d)(3)(A) and (B) of this title),
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity by changing "agrees to indemnify" to "agrees to potentially indemnify" and including phrases like "some losses" and "certain costs." The addition of "The determination of indemnification shall be at the Adviser's sole discretion" further weakens the indemnification clause, making it discretionary. The Law Explanation states that Section 17(h) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 prohibits exculpation for actions constituting willful misfeasance, bad faith, gross negligence, or reckless disregard of duty. The modified text, by making indemnification discretionary and weakening the language regarding gross negligence, creates ambiguity that could be interpreted as attempting to limit liability for actions that should not be exculpated under the Investment Company Act. This directly contradicts the principle that certain liabilities cannot be waived or made discretionary.
